text: Three trocars are inserted into the lateral abdomen, providing access for the robotic instruments during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses a five millimeter optical trocar to gain initial intraperitoneal access, inserting the trocar through the abdominal wall to visualize the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon insufflates the abdominal cavity to fifteen millimeters of mercury pressure, followed by an initial laparoscopic inspection of the intraperitoneal space.
The surgeon places a twelve millimeter trocar under direct vision in the abdominal cavity, preparing for further surgical access.
An eight millimeter robotic trocar is placed in the left lower quadrant of the abdomen, enhancing the surgical setup for robotic hernia repair.
The surgeon replaces the five millimeter optical trocar with an eight millimeter robotic trocar in the abdominal cavity, maintaining access for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon reduces the omentum to reveal the hernia defect, providing a clear view of the affected area.
The laparoscopic view reveals the presence of two distinct hernia defects within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon visually identifies the hernia on the left side as an umbilical hernia, with the defect clearly exposed.
The surgeon reduces the omentum from the incisional hernia site, which is a result of a previous laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon defines the extent of the hernia and selects an area at least five centimeters proximal to the hernia defect to incise the peritoneum.
The surgeon develops a preperitoneal flap, similar to the technique used in transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) inguinal hernia repairs, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgical team approaches the umbilical hernia defect, focusing on the area around the umbilicus to address the hernia.
The surgeon reduces preperitoneal fat from the umbilical hernia defect, clearing the area around the hernia site.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the peritoneal flap distal to the umbilical hernia defect, carefully separating the peritoneal tissue from underlying structures.

The surgeon switches the laparoscopic camera angle from thirty degrees up to thirty degrees down to enhance visibility and continue the dissection of the peritoneal flap around the umbilical hernia defect.
The surgeon meticulously continues the dissection around the umbilical hernia defect, carefully avoiding significant disruption of the peritoneum.
The surgeon completes the dissection of an adequate flap to accommodate the mesh and begins closing the umbilical hernia defects.
The surgeon uses an OV-lock suture to close the hernia defects, securing the tissue layers together.
After closing the incisional hernia defect, the surgeon uses a second suture to close the umbilical hernia defect, ensuring both areas are securely repaired.
The surgeon incorporates a bite of subcutaneous skin into the suture repair to recreate the inverted umbilicus, ensuring the skin is properly aligned and secured.
The surgeon re-approximates small disruptions in the peritoneum using III-O Vicryl suture, carefully stitching the tissue to restore continuity.
The assistant carefully guides the mesh into the intraperitoneal cavity, preparing it for placement over the hernia defect.
The Ethicon Physiomesh is visible, positioned within the surgical field as part of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses an Ethicon secure strap tacker to attach the Physiomesh to the abdominal wall, ensuring the mesh is firmly secured in place.
The surgeon uses a secure strap stapler to re-approximate the peritoneum, visibly aligning and securing the tissue layers within the abdominal cavity.
